questions.passage:
When I entered art school, I carried a convenient theory of originality. The serious artist, I thought, looked inward; the timid one looked sideways. Copying belonged to childhood exercises, commercial workshops, and those diligent painters who could reproduce a face without ever discovering a vision. I had absorbed the romance of the untouched impulse so completely that even close study of another painter felt faintly compromising. Admiration was permissible, but imitation seemed like a confession of insufficiency.

My first instructor answered this vanity with an assignment. We were to choose a small section of an older painting and reproduce it at the same scale. No tracing, no projected outline, no attempt to complete the whole image. I selected a sleeve whose dark folds appeared simple enough to finish quickly. Three days later, my copy looked like polished leather, while the painted sleeve retained the weight of cloth and the uncertainty of shadow. What I had called black contained green, red, and a dry gray dragged across the surface. The edge I had drawn crisply was produced by adjacent tones that never quite met. The original had not hidden a secret technique from me; I had hidden its decisions from myself by naming their result too soon.

Copying is valuable because it converts admiration into a sequence of accountable guesses. To proceed, the copier must decide where a form begins, which relation matters, and what can be sacrificed without collapse. Every answer can be compared with a stubborn visual fact. This differs from passive reverence. A person may stand before a painting and praise its subtlety while remaining unable to identify a single subtle decision. The copy exposes such vagueness. It gives error a surface.

Of course, exact duplication is impossible. Pigments age, brushes differ, and one hand cannot recover the pressure of another. But impossibility is not a defect in the exercise; it is the source of its intelligence. The gap between the model and the copy records a chain of misunderstandings. A misplaced highlight may reveal that the copier mistook reflected light for local color. A flattened figure may show that contour was observed while internal proportion was ignored. The failed resemblance is diagnostic, not merely disappointing.

Critics of copying fear obedience. They imagine students learning to borrow a master’s solutions rather than forming judgments of their own. That can happen when the exercise rewards resemblance alone. Yet a rigorous copy does not end with the announcement that one version looks more accurate. It asks what produced the difference and whether the original solution remains persuasive under reconstruction. Sometimes the most faithful inquiry ends in disagreement. One discovers that a celebrated transition is evasive, that an elegant pose depends on anatomical improbability, or that a color effect cannot survive outside a narrow arrangement. Copying can puncture prestige as efficiently as it can deepen respect.

Years after that first sleeve, I no longer think originality begins where imitation stops. Originality begins when seeing becomes exact enough to support refusal. A painter who has never reconstructed another person’s choices may mistake preference for independence. The copier, forced to inhabit decisions without being able to repeat them perfectly, learns a harsher freedom: to know what has been inherited, what has been misunderstood, and what must be done otherwise. The purpose of copying is not to produce a second original. It is to make unearned certainty difficult.
questions.stem: Which statement best expresses the passage's main idea?

choices.A: Artistic originality depends on rejecting the techniques of established painters.
choices.B: Copying can reveal hidden decisions and prepare the ground for informed independence.
choices.C: Successful imitation requires materials that closely match those of the original work.
choices.D: Studio instruction should emphasize technical accuracy over personal interpretation.

explanations.A: The author argues that informed refusal can emerge from reconstructing inherited choices rather than from avoiding them.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage presents copying as a diagnostic practice that exposes artistic decisions, clarifies error, and enables accountable disagreement.
explanations.C: Differences in materials help make exact duplication impossible, but the exercise remains valuable precisely because of that gap.
explanations.D: Accuracy serves inquiry in the passage and is not elevated above judgment or interpretation.

questions.stem: The account of copying the painted sleeve primarily functions to

choices.A: show how imitation uncovered decisions the author had failed to perceive.
choices.B: demonstrate that older painters possessed superior knowledge of color mixing.
choices.C: explain why art students should begin with clothing rather than faces.
choices.D: illustrate the practical difficulty of obtaining historically accurate pigments.

explanations.A: Correct. The failed sleeve reveals that the author had reduced complex relations of color and edge to premature labels such as black and crisp.
explanations.B: The episode contrasts forms of attention and does not rank entire generations of painters by technical knowledge.
explanations.C: The sleeve is a personal episode rather than a recommended sequence for studio instruction.
explanations.D: Material difference appears later as one reason duplication is impossible and is not the focus of the anecdote.

questions.stem: Which claim is most strongly implied by the passage?

choices.A: Admiration becomes reliable only after a work has been copied successfully.
choices.B: Prestigious artworks usually conceal major technical weaknesses.
choices.C: A poor resemblance proves that the copier lacks visual talent.
choices.D: Errors in a copy can identify the assumptions that produced them.

explanations.A: The author values reconstruction but never makes successful copying a requirement for justified admiration.
explanations.B: Copying may expose weaknesses in some celebrated works, but the passage does not portray such weaknesses as usual.
explanations.C: Failed resemblance is treated as evidence about specific misunderstandings rather than as a verdict on general talent.
explanations.D: Correct. The author describes misplaced highlights and flattened figures as records of mistaken judgments about color, contour, and proportion.

questions.stem: A writing instructor asks students to reproduce the structure of an admired essay and then identify where their versions diverge. This practice most closely reflects the author's view because it

choices.A: encourages students to adopt a proven style before developing their own.
choices.B: treats formal resemblance as the final measure of successful learning.
choices.C: uses attempted reconstruction to expose choices and misunderstandings.
choices.D: protects admired works from criticism until their methods are mastered.

explanations.A: The author rejects copying as simple adoption and emphasizes judgment about what should be retained or refused.
explanations.B: Resemblance begins the inquiry but does not end it; the differences require explanation.
explanations.C: Correct. Rebuilding a structure and studying divergence parallels the passage’s account of making hidden decisions and errors visible.
explanations.D: The passage allows reconstruction to deepen respect or puncture prestige, so admired works are not shielded from criticism.

questions.stem: Which finding would most strengthen the author's argument?

choices.A: Students who analyze failed copies later explain original artworks with greater specificity.
choices.B: Students prefer copying assignments that involve famous works rather than unfamiliar ones.
choices.C: Copies made with modern pigments are easier to preserve than the original paintings.
choices.D: Viewers often rate a copy highly when they have never seen the original work.

explanations.A: Correct. Greater specificity after analyzing failure would support the claim that copying converts vague admiration into accountable understanding of artistic decisions.
explanations.B: A preference for famous models would reveal student taste without showing that copying improves perception or judgment.
explanations.C: Preservation advantages concern material durability rather than the diagnostic value of reconstruction.
explanations.D: Mistaking a copy for a strong work without comparison would not show that the exercise clarifies decisions.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward copying is best described as

choices.A: reluctant approval based on its usefulness for beginners.
choices.B: nostalgic attachment to a declining studio tradition.
choices.C: forceful endorsement of its critical and diagnostic value.
choices.D: neutral interest in its technical limitations.

explanations.A: The author’s approval extends beyond beginners and culminates in a broad claim about informed artistic freedom.
explanations.B: The passage is autobiographical but does not mourn the disappearance of an older educational custom.
explanations.C: Correct. The author presents copying as a demanding method that exposes error, disciplines admiration, and enables genuine disagreement.
explanations.D: Technical limitations are interpreted as productive rather than described with neutrality.
